embeds_html:
SenseCAP Solar Node P1 for Meshtastic
It is an economical solar-powered comms node or repeater based on Meshtastic. It meets basic comms needs in no - network zones. Unlike P1-Pro, P1 lacks a built-in GPS module and four 18650 batteries, yet keeps core comms. It's perfect for budget-constrained scenarios or those not needing precise location, like environmental monitoring and short ...

messages.author: ThatGuy
messages.text: Inside the attic probably isn't going to work well. Most of these devices are transmitting at some fraction of a single watt of power. Even foliage degrades the signal, particularly if it's wet. It's just a function of this part of the spectrum. Line of sight is critical. You'll lose significant amounts of signal if you're shooting through insulation board, roof decking, shingles/tiles and then to the outside world. Suggest you try mounting it outside, either under an eave up high with the antenna sticking out, or if in a weatherproof enclosure, up in a tree or up a pole. Also, invest in a good antenna. Hope this helps. (edited)
messages.content_html: Inside the attic probably isn't going to work well. Most of these devices are transmitting at some fraction of a single watt of power. Even foliage degrades the signal, particularly if it's wet. It's just a function of this part of the spectrum. Line of sight is critical. You'll lose significant amounts of signal if you're shooting through insulation board, roof decking, shingles/tiles and then to the outside world. Suggest you try mounting it outside, either under an eave up high with the antenna sticking out, or if in a weatherproof enclosure, up in a tree or up a pole. Also, invest in a good antenna. Hope this helps. (edited)
messages.reply_html:
ChiefExecutiveGamer
I barely get out of my neighborhood and can’t see the repeater in my attic.

messages.author: Larnth
messages.text: I'm struggling to find the benefit of meshcore in our area with how little traffic I expect us to have (compared to, say, PNW...) When I flash to meshtastic I get nodes popping up daily, giving me (a perhaps false) sense that I'm connecting to plenty of local people in a living and breathing mesh But when I go to Meshcore I can't seem to ever find anyone at my house or on my commute to work (or at my work office which is near the Promenade)
messages.content_html: I'm struggling to find the benefit of meshcore in our area with how little traffic I expect us to have (compared to, say, PNW...) When I flash to meshtastic I get nodes popping up daily, giving me (a perhaps false) sense that I'm connecting to plenty of local people in a living and breathing mesh But when I go to Meshcore I can't seem to ever find anyone at my house or on my commute to work (or at my work office which is near the Promenade)

messages.author: Robert - N5QM
messages.text: I know it doesn't generally come up in conversation, but I ask you guys to consider the maintainability of each system. There is where I, as an infrastructure operator, see distinct differences. Each area is different, but specifically I am looking to operate solar powered infrastructure with little to no external dependancies such that it can be used when there is no internet or electricity. Historically if you have a tower mounted NRF device on MT, that means climbing the tower to upgrade firmware or risk bricking the device via a bluetooth OTA upgrade. On the MC side, there is a bootloader release that not only prevents bricking the device by restarting in the bluetooth OTA(DFU) mode if the OTA fails, but also makes some improvements on the speed of the bluetooth transfer. On the ESP32 side with MC you can do an OTA by remotely triggering integrated WIFI system to activate in AP mode and then connecting to that AP and uploading the new firmware. MT is catching up on this as they are actively working on the OTA process for the ESP. That being said, there is little advantage to running a power hungry ESP device as an infrastructure node in comparison to the efficiency of the NRF based devices.
messages.content_html: I know it doesn't generally come up in conversation, but I ask you guys to consider the maintainability of each system. There is where I, as an infrastructure operator, see distinct differences. Each area is different, but specifically I am looking to operate solar powered infrastructure with little to no external dependancies such that it can be used when there is no internet or electricity. Historically if you have a tower mounted NRF device on MT, that means climbing the tower to upgrade firmware or risk bricking the device via a bluetooth OTA upgrade. On the MC side, there is a bootloader release that not only prevents bricking the device by restarting in the bluetooth OTA(DFU) mode if the OTA fails, but also makes some improvements on the speed of the bluetooth transfer. On the ESP32 side with MC you can do an OTA by remotely triggering integrated WIFI system to activate in AP mode and then connecting to that AP and uploading the new firmware. MT is catching up on this as they are actively working on the OTA process for the ESP. That being said, there is little advantage to running a power hungry ESP device as an infrastructure node in comparison to the efficiency of the NRF based devices.

messages.author: magwich.
messages.text: So do you run both or prefer MC or MT? Thank you!
messages.content_html: So do you run both or prefer MC or MT? Thank you!
messages.reply_html:
Robert - N5QM
I know it doesn't generally come up in conversation, but I ask you guys to consider the maintainability of each system. There is where I, as an infrastructure operator, see distinct differences. Each area is different, but specifically I am looking to operate solar powered infrastructure with little to no external dependancies such that it can be used when there is no internet or electricity. Historically if you have a tower mounted NRF device on MT, that means climbing the tower to upgrade firmware or risk bricking the device via a bluetooth OTA upgrade. On the MC side, there is a bootloader release that not only prevents bricking the device by restarting in the bluetooth OTA(DFU) mode if the OTA fails, but also makes some improvements on the speed of the bluetooth transfer. On the ESP32 side with MC you can do an OTA by remotely triggering integrated WIFI system to activate in AP mode and then connecting to that AP and uploading the new firmware. MT is catching up on this as they are actively working on the OTA process for the ESP. That being said, there is little advantage to running a power hungry ESP device as an infrastructure node in comparison to the efficiency of the NRF based devices.

messages.author: Robert - N5QM
messages.text: That is a difficult question to answer. From a technical perspective I "prefer" MC because of the routing and maintainability of the infrastructure. I think MT will catch up eventually on the technical side of things, but trying to make significant changes while maintaining backwards compatability is going to be a difficult challenge that will likely slow down progress. That being said, overcoming the head start that MT has in regards to the number of users is likely going to be the biggest challenge for MC.
messages.content_html: That is a difficult question to answer. From a technical perspective I "prefer" MC because of the routing and maintainability of the infrastructure. I think MT will catch up eventually on the technical side of things, but trying to make significant changes while maintaining backwards compatability is going to be a difficult challenge that will likely slow down progress. That being said, overcoming the head start that MT has in regards to the number of users is likely going to be the biggest challenge for MC.
messages.reply_html:
magwich.
So do you run both or prefer MC or MT? Thank you!

messages.author: extra5gold
messages.text: @Robert - N5QM and I (and a few others) have been debating/discussing/testing/learning on this for a while now. I don't think there is a clear answer. There are specific scenarios where it is clear that there is a better choice. With most scenarios, there are pros and cons to them both. I will also say that I'm hesitant to really debate it too much in the community hosted by either platform.
messages.content_html: @Robert - N5QM and I (and a few others) have been debating/discussing/testing/learning on this for a while now. I don't think there is a clear answer. There are specific scenarios where it is clear that there is a better choice. With most scenarios, there are pros and cons to them both. I will also say that I'm hesitant to really debate it too much in the community hosted by either platform.

messages.author: extra5gold
messages.text: We already have a tuned cavity filter on Shinall (MT). Interference is basically on frequency so there is no filtering it out. I intend to go do some spectrum analysis in the node antenna to see if there are quiet places in band that we could move to get away from the noise.
messages.content_html: We already have a tuned cavity filter on Shinall (MT). Interference is basically on frequency so there is no filtering it out. I intend to go do some spectrum analysis in the node antenna to see if there are quiet places in band that we could move to get away from the noise.
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.reply_html:
White5Actual
Yeah I’m in Benton/Bryant and there is nothing my way I’ve seen. I work downtown in one of the high rise buildings and may see about putting a repeater on top and working outwards from there.

messages.author: White5Actual
messages.text: Yeah I think I’ve seen your Meshtastic nodes. I’m off of Northlake.
messages.content_html: Yeah I think I’ve seen your Meshtastic nodes. I’m off of Northlake.
messages.reply_html:
extra5gold
Also in Saline county. I live out Springhill Road north of Bryant.

messages.author: extra5gold
messages.text: Ok cool. Not far from me then. I’m in Springhill Farms.
messages.content_html: Ok cool. Not far from me then. I’m in Springhill Farms.

messages.author: White5Actual
messages.text: Yep we are pretty close! Are you using both MC and MT? I just went on a trip to San Diego and their MC network was so much more reliable than the MT network. Made me want to do a switch.
messages.content_html: Yep we are pretty close! Are you using both MC and MT? I just went on a trip to San Diego and their MC network was so much more reliable than the MT network. Made me want to do a switch.

messages.author: extra5gold
messages.text: I have an MC companion and have flashed a few nodes to MC and back. Mostly on MT though.
messages.content_html: I have an MC companion and have flashed a few nodes to MC and back. Mostly on MT though.

messages.author: themachine
messages.text: Ya it’s impressive how quick it cuts off.
messages.content_html: Ya it’s impressive how quick it cuts off.

messages.content_html: Are you guys seeing MC increase much in the central Arkansas area?

extra5gold
I have an MC companion and have flashed a few nodes to MC and back. Mostly on MT though.

messages.author: extra5gold

messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: the meshcore dev team have moved to a new discord server. details and reasons are here: https://blog.meshcore.io/2026/04/23/the-split Please create a new thread for your region in the https://discord.com/channels/1495203904898728149/1495412453150818585 for your region on the new meshcore.io discord server. https://meshcore.gg
